examples.title: Creating Tables with Keys
examples.description: SQL DDL statements demonstrating PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, and UNIQUE (Alternate Key) constraints
examples.code:
-- Department Table
CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Location VARCHAR(50)
);

-- Employee Table with Primary Key, Foreign Key, and Alternate Key
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmpID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,  -- Alternate Key
    Phone VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE,   -- Alternate Key
    Salary DECIMAL(10,2),
    DeptID INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
        ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
examples.explanation: This SQL creates two tables demonstrating: PRIMARY KEY (DeptID, EmpID), FOREIGN KEY (Employee.DeptID references Department.DeptID), UNIQUE constraints (Email, Phone serve as Alternate Keys). ON DELETE SET NULL maintains referential integrity.

examples.title: Python Class for Relation and Keys
examples.description: Object-oriented representation of a Relation with key validation
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates relation properties: degree (number of attributes) and cardinality (number of tuples). The find_keys method identifies super keys containing the primary key.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n) for generating all super keys where n = number of non-PK attributes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(t) where t = number of tuples

examples.title: Key Selection Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode to find candidate keys from a set of attributes using functional dependencies
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FindCandidateKeys
INPUT: Relation R with attributes A, set of Functional Dependencies F
OUTPUT: Set of Candidate Keys CK
BEGIN
    // Step 1: Find attributes that never appear on RHS (mandatory in keys)
    mandatoryAttrs ← attributes not in any FD RHS
    
    // Step 2: Start with mandatory attributes
    currentKey ← mandatoryAttrs
    
    // Step 3: Try adding optional attributes until closure = all attributes
    FOR each optionalAttr IN optionalAttributes DO
        IF closure(currentKey ∪ {optionalAttr}, F) = allAttributes THEN
            // Check if minimal (no proper subset is a key)
            isMinimal ← TRUE
            FOR each attr IN currentKey DO
                IF closure(currentKey - {attr}, F) = allAttributes THEN
                    isMinimal ← FALSE
                    BREAK
                END IF
            END FOR
            IF isMinimal THEN
                ADD currentKey TO candidateKeys
            END IF
            currentKey ← currentKey ∪ {optionalAttr}
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN candidateKeys
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm finds all candidate keys using functional dependencies. It first identifies mandatory attributes (those not on RHS of any FD), then tests combinations to find minimal keys whose closure equals all attributes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n × n × FD_evaluation) where n = number of attributes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(2^k) where k = number of candidate keys found

examples.title: C Structure for Relation with Key Validation
examples.description: C program demonstrating relation structure and key constraint validation
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates key validation concepts: checking primary key uniqueness (entity integrity) and foreign key referential integrity validation. Shows how databases enforce key constraints programmatically.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for uniqueness and referential checks
